title: Executive Order 11785—Amending Executive Order No. 10450, as Amended, Relating to Security Requirements for Government Employment, and for Other Purposes

flags.justification: This revokes an organization-designation mechanism and Sec. 2 affirmatively prohibits the Attorney General, the Subversive Activities Control Board, or any other agency from designating organizations going forward — removing rather than concentrating power.
A rights-narrowing restriction is being removed, not a new program created; no new ambiguity about responsibility is introduced.

Multiple related provisions (revoking the designation mechanism, prohibiting future designations, narrowing the disqualification standard, revoking a related 1971 order) all coherently serve one purpose: dismantling and narrowing the loyalty-security apparatus.

Sec. 3's revised standard requires 'knowing membership with the specific intent of furthering the aims of' an organization that 'unlawfully advocates or practices' violence or overthrow — substantially more precise than a bare membership-in-a-listed-organization standard.
This closes off a mechanism (guilt-by-association listing with no hearing process) that itself carried real chilling-effect concerns, rather than creating a new one.
Modifies a standing personnel-security framework, evergreen by nature.
This removes a mechanism (the Attorney General's list) that itself operated without a hearing or appeal process — improving rather than preempting oversight and due process.
No ongoing compliance regime is created by this order; it narrows an existing one.
This narrows rather than expands executive authority; no broad or reusable new authority claim is made.
No second agency's competing mandate is implicated.
This narrows a substantive disqualification standard rather than creating exemptions from coverage.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 0/16 = 0.0%. One of Nixon's last orders (issued roughly two months before his resignation), this dismantles the Attorney General's List — a notorious McCarthy-era loyalty-security mechanism — and narrows the disqualification standard to require specific intent and unlawful violence-advocacy, aligning with subsequent First Amendment jurisprudence. A genuinely rights-expanding reform, and the instrument correctly registers it as clean and low-risk precisely because it narrows rather than concentrates governmental power.
